fact: A forty-mile-per-hour wind caused the bridge's fatal oscillations on November 7, 1940
source: Washington State Department of Transportation – Tacoma Narrows Bridge history

fact: The bridge deck was only thirty-nine feet wide with two lanes
source: "Tacoma Narrows Bridge" – Wikipedia, citing original engineering specifications

fact: The bridge was nicknamed 'Galloping Gertie' due to its persistent vertical oscillations
source: American Society of Civil Engineers – Tacoma Narrows Bridge case study

fact: The deck tilted up to approximately forty-five degrees during the final torsional oscillations before collapse
source: Physics of the Tacoma Narrows Bridge Collapse – University of Washington archival film analysis; widely documented in engineering literature

fact: A six-hundred-foot section of the center span broke free and fell into Puget Sound
source: "Catastrophe to Triumph: Bridges of the Tacoma Narrows" by Richard S. Hobbs (2006); the center span was 2,800 ft total and collapsed in large sections

fact: The collapse was caused by a moderate sustained wind (not a major storm), through aeroelastic flutter
source: Federal Works Agency report on the Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse (1941); widely documented
